success_surface: The agent successfully identified blank rows within a specified range and deleted them using proper iteration order while preserving file integrity and validating results.
effective_behaviors: Inspecting complete spreadsheet structure before determining manipulation scope
Defining target ranges based on provided answer_position metadata
Deleting multiple rows in reverse (bottom-to-top) order to prevent index shifting errors
Creating file backups before any modification operations
Validating cell values by checking both None types and whitespace-only strings
Verifying row content across all columns before deletion to preserve data integrity
Printing detailed execution logs showing identified targets and completed actions
evidence: Agent printed all spreadsheet data to identify exact row positions before coding deletion logic
Used start_row=3, end_row=24 derived from 'Sheet1'!A3:A24 answer_position
Iterated through blank_rows list with reversed() to delete from bottom to top
Used shutil.copy() to duplicate input_path before load_workbook()
Checked if cell_value is None or str(cell_value).strip() == '' for blank detection
Looped through ws.max_column to verify other columns had content before row deletion
Printed each deleted row number and final verification of remaining values in Column A

memory_items.title: Pre-Manipulation Data Inspection
memory_items.lesson: Always inspect the full worksheet structure and content distribution before implementing row/column modifications
memory_items.generalization: When dealing with unknown spreadsheet data, perform a complete scan to map existing values and their positions. This reveals edge cases like empty header regions, intermittent blank sections, and actual data boundaries that inform correct range selection.

memory_items.title: Reverse Iteration for Bulk Deletions
memory_items.lesson: When deleting multiple rows, always process them in reverse order (bottom to top)
memory_items.generalization: Row deletion shifts indices downward. Processing from highest row index to lowest prevents skipping subsequent deletions because previously-deleted rows haven't yet affected the indices you're iterating toward. Store target indices first, then reverse-sort before deletion.

memory_items.title: Multi-Column Validation Before Row Deletion
memory_items.lesson: Before deleting a row based on one column's emptiness, verify whether other columns contain meaningful data
memory_items.generalization: A cell being blank in a target column doesn't mean the entire row should be removed. Check adjacent columns in the same row to ensure no cross-column dependencies exist. Delete only if truly empty or confirmed safe per task requirements.

memory_items.title: Metadata-Driven Range Scoping
memory_items.lesson: Extract target ranges from task metadata (answer_position, instruction) rather than assuming full-sheet operations
memory_items.generalization: Tasks often specify precise cell ranges where modifications are expected. Parse these position markers to limit your operations to relevant areas, avoiding unnecessary processing and preventing unintended side effects on untouched regions.

memory_items.title: Execution Logging and Post-Action Validation
memory_items.lesson: Document every transformation step and verify final state matches expectations
memory_items.generalization: Print collected targets, action counts, and sample outputs after modifications. Compare pre/post row counts, spot-check specific cells, and confirm data alignment. This creates an audit trail and catches off-by-one or scope errors before saving.

edits.content:
## 6. Trace-Derived Lessons

### Pre-Manipulation Data Inspection
- Always inspect the full worksheet structure and content distribution before implementing row/column modifications. Perform a complete scan to map existing values and their positions, revealing edge cases like empty header regions, intermittent blank sections, and actual data boundaries.

### Reverse Iteration for Bulk Deletions
- When deleting multiple rows, always store target indices first, then process them in reverse order (highest to lowest). Row deletion shifts indices downward; reverse iteration prevents skipping subsequent deletions because previously-deleted rows haven't yet affected the indices you're targeting.

### Multi-Column Validation Before Row Deletion
- Before deleting a row based on one column's emptiness, verify whether other columns contain meaningful data. A cell being blank in a target column doesn't mean the entire row should be removed. Check adjacent columns in the same row to ensure no cross-column dependencies exist.

### Metadata-Driven Range Scoping
- Extract target ranges from task metadata (answer_position, instruction) rather than assuming full-sheet operations. Tasks often specify precise cell ranges where modifications are expected. Parse these position markers to limit your operations to relevant areas.

### Execution Logging and Post-Action Validation
- Document every transformation step and verify final state matches expectations. Print collected targets, action counts, and sample outputs after modifications. Compare pre/post row counts, spot-check specific cells, and confirm data alignment before saving.
